coredata.dc:title: Thermoelectric properties of hexagonal graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yonghong
Liang, Qi-Feng
Zhao, Hui
Wu, Chang-Qin
coredata.dc:description:
By using the atomistic nonequilibrium Greenʼs function method, we investigate the thermoelectric properties of graphene nanoribbons in the presence of two constrictions (or hexagonal graphene quantum dots). With decreasing widths of the constrictions, the thermal conductance of the nanoribbon can be reduced largely while
S 2
G e
(S is the Seebeck coefficient and
G e
is the electronic conductance) remains still high as compared with the results of the pristine nanoribbon. Thus, the thermoelectric figure of merit ZT can be enhanced largely. In fact, in the presence of narrowest constrictions the ZT values of the zigzag quantum dots can exceed one at room temperature, while the ZT values of the armchair quantum dots may be close to one, depending on the size of the dot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermoelectric property
Hexagonal graphene quantum dot
ZT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(Color online.) (a) Phonon transmission for the ZGNR (
N z
= 8 ) with the narrowest constrictions (thick line). As a comparison, transmission of the pristine ZGNR is also shown (thin line). (b) Phonon thermal conductance of the nanoribbon with the narrowest constrictions (thick line) and the pristine ZGNR (thin line). The inset shows the thermal conductance of ZGNRs with the narrowest constrictions when the width (
N z
) is varied. The temperature is T =300.

Thermoelectric properties of a ZGNR (
N z
= 14 ) with the narrowest constrictions. The temperature is T =300 K. (a), (b), (c), and (d) show the thermoelectric figure of merit ZT, thermopower S, electron transmission, and electron thermal conductance
κ e
, respectively.

(Color online.) (a) Maximal ZT value of the ZGNR (
N z
= 14 ) as a function of the constriction width (
N c
/
N z
). (b) and (c) show the corresponding
S 2
G e
and the thermal conductance, respectively, when ZT takes the maximal value.

(Color online.) (a) Maximal ZT values of ZGNRs with different widths (
N z
) in the presence of the narrowest constrictions (solid squares). (b) and (c) show the corresponding
S 2
G e
and the thermal conductance, respectively (solid diamonds and triangles). As a comparison, results for the pristine ZGNRs are also shown (empty symbols).

(Color online.) Phonon thermal conductance of the AGNR (
N a
= 11 ) with the narrowest constrictions (full line). The result for the pristine AGNR is also shown as a comparison (dashed line). The inset shows the thermal conductance of AGNRs with the narrowest constrictions when the width (
N a
) is varied. The temperature is T =300.

(Color online.) (a) shows the maximal ZT value of the AGNR (
N a
= 23 ) as a function of the constriction width (
N c
/
N a
). (b) and (c) show the corresponding
S 2
G e
and the thermal conductance, respectively, when ZT takes the maximal value.

(Color online.) (a) Maximal ZT values of AGNRs with different widths (
N a
) in the presence of the narrowest constrictions (solid squares). (b) and (c) show the corresponding
S 2
G e
and the thermal conductance, respectively (solid diamonds and triangles). As a comparison, results for the pristine AGNRs are also shown (empty symbols).

head.ce:title: Thermoelectric properties of hexagonal graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shaoxing University, Shaoxing 312000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tongji University, Shanghai 200092, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
By using the atomistic nonequilibrium Greenʼs function method, we investigate the thermoelectric properties of graphene nanoribbons in the presence of two constrictions (or hexagonal graphene quantum dots). With decreasing widths of the constrictions, the thermal conductance of the nanoribbon can be reduced largely while
S 2
G e
(S is the Seebeck coefficient and
G e
is the electronic conductance) remains still high as compared with the results of the pristine nanoribbon. Thus, the thermoelectric figure of merit ZT can be enhanced largely. In fact, in the presence of narrowest constrictions the ZT values of the zigzag quantum dots can exceed one at room temperature, while the ZT values of the armchair quantum dots may be close to one, depending on the size of the dot.

► We study thermoelectric properties of hexagonal graphene quantum dots. ► By point contacts to two leads, the thermal conductance can be reduced greatly while keeping the power factor still high. ► Thermoelectric figure of merit ( ZT ) can exceed unity.

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene [1,2] is a one-atom thick s
p 2
-bonded carbon sheet with a honeycomb lattice structure. In the past few years, graphene and graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) have drawn extensive attention due to their intriguing properties, e.g., the ultra-high electron mobility [2] and the tunable band gap and magnetic properties by the size and edge chirality [3–5]. These unusual electronic properties could find utility in many applications, particularly in nanoelectronic devices [6]. Meanwhile, graphene also has remarkable thermal properties that may be used in thermal management and thermoelectric energy conversion [7,8]. In fact, the measured value of thermal conductivity of graphene that is dominated by phonons is up to 3000–5000 W/mK [9–11], among the highest values of known materials.

For thermoelectric energy conversion, however, a low thermal conductance is required to increase the efficiency of a thermoelectric device. The efficiency is characterized by the figure of merit ZT =
S 2
G e
T / κ , where S is the Seebeck coefficient (or thermopower),
G e
is the electronic conductance, T is the temperature, and κ is the total thermal conductance including contributions from both phonons and electrons. The ideal thermoelectric material is the so-called “phonon glass–electron crystal” with a high thermopower S and a low thermal conductance κ. Indeed a high thermopower value up to 80 μV/K was reported for graphene [12,13]. Then the natural question is whether we can design specific structures where phonon thermal conductance is degraded while the electronic conductance and the thermopower are still high. In this context, different structures of graphene have been examined, including various nanoribbons [14–19], graphene antidot lattices [20,21], graphene junctions and chevron-type GNRs [22], molecular devices [23], etc. In particular, Sevinçli et al. investigated the thermoelectric properties in edge-disordered zigzag GNRs [18]. They predicted that ZT of zigzag GNRs with edge disorder can exceed 3 in the diffusive limit.

In this Letter, we investigate the thermoelectric properties of graphene nanoribbons in the presence of two constrictions. This structure can be also viewed as a graphene quantum dot [24]. The structure may formed by carving narrow constrictions on pristine GNRs. Our results indicate that thermoelectric figure of merit in GNRs with constrictions can be enhanced greatly, being up to one (or even up to two in certain case), since narrow constrictions can reduce the thermal conductance efficiently while keeping the power factor (
S 2
G e
) still high.

To fabricate these structures, the technology to control precisely the ribbon edges at the atomic scale is required. Indeed, rapid progress in this direction has been achieved. Recently, a new process was developed to control the chemical narrowing of graphene beyond the capability of conventional top-down lithography [25]. In addition, another recent work introduced a bottom-up approach to atomically precise fabrication of GNRs [26], which allows fabrication of ribbons with complex shapes. Motivated by the advance of fabrication technique, an investigation on thermoelectric properties of various confined structures of graphene will be of interest and we wish to draw attention to thermoelectric properties of these nanostructures.

The structures of GNRs we study are shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1(a) shows a zigzag GNR (ZGNR) in the presence of two narrowest constrictions. The central part between the two constrictions can be also viewed as a hexagonal graphene quantum dot with a side length L. In the following, we will keep the side length along the longitudinal direction (electron or heat current direction) fixed while other side lengths may be changeable, depending on the widths of constrictions. The left and the right parts are two semi-infinite pristine graphene nanoribbons acting as two leads. In the case of narrowest constrictions, the central region and the lead form a point contact. The width of the ZGNR (in the absence of constrictions) can be identified by the number of zigzag chains
N z
, and in Fig. 1(a) the width is
N z
= 10 . The number of carbon atom pairs in the box of Fig. 1(a),
N c
, indicates the width of the constriction. Fig. 1(b) shows an armchair GNR (AGNR) in the presence of two narrowest constrictions. In this case, the width of the AGNR (in the absence of constrictions) may be identified by the number of atom pairs,
N a
, in a unit cell [
N a
= 11 in Fig. 1(b)].

To study the thermoelectric properties, we model the electronic transport properties by the widely used nearest-neighbor π-orbital tight binding Hamiltonian,
(1)
H = − t
∑
〈 i , j 〉
c i †
c j
+ H.c. ,
where the summation is over all pairs of nearest-neighbor atoms, and the hopping energy t = 2.7
eV is used in this Letter. We calculate the electronic current using the standard nonequilibrium Greenʼs function (NEGF) formalism [27,28]. The electronic current is then given by the Landauer formula,
(2)
I =
2 e
h
∫
− ∞
∞
d E
T e
( E )
[ f ( E ,
μ L
) − f ( E ,
μ R
) ]
,
where
T e
( E ) is the electron transmission, and f ( E ,
μ
L , R
) = 1 / ( exp [ ( E −
μ
L , R
) /
k B
T ] + 1 ) is the Fermi–Dirac distribution function in the left and right leads with chemical potentials
μ
L , R
. The electronic conductance is
(3)
G e
( μ ) =
2
e 2
h
∫
− ∞
∞
d E
T e
( E )
( −
∂ f ( E , μ )
∂ E
)
=
e 2
L 0
,
where the function
L m
( μ ) is defined as
(4)
L m
( μ ) =
2 h
∫
− ∞
∞
d E
T e
( E )
( E − μ )
m
( −
∂ f ( E , μ )
∂ E
)
.
The thermopower S and electron thermal conductance
κ e
can be shown to be
(5)
S =
1
e T
L 1
L 0
and
(6)
κ e
=
1 T
(
L 2
−
L 1 2
L 0
)
,
respectively.

The phonon transport is modeled by a fourth-nearest-neighbor force constant (4NNFC) model, which yields phonon dispersions in agreement with first-principles results for graphene and carbon nanotubes [22,29]. The 12 parameters of the 4NNFC model we used are adopted from Ref. [29]. These parameters yields a considerable improvement in the overall phonon dispersion of graphene as compared to the parameters given in Ref. [30], and in particular the acoustic modes are consistent well with ab initio data [29]. The phonon thermal current can be calculated from the phonon transmission function
T
p h
as [31]
(7)
J
p h
( T ) =
ℏ
2 π
∫ 0 ∞
d ω
ω
T
p h
( ω )
[
n B
(
T L
) −
n B
(
T R
) ]
,
where
n B
(
T
L , R
) =
(
e
ℏ ω /
k B
T
L , R
− 1 )
− 1
is the Bose–Einstein distribution and
T
L , R
is the left (right) bath temperature. In the linear response regime, the phonon thermal conductance is then
(8)
κ
p h
( T ) =
ℏ 2
2 π
k B
T 2
∫ 0 ∞
d ω
ω 2
T
p h
( ω )
e
ℏ ω /
k B
T
(
e
ℏ ω /
k B
T
− 1 )
2
.

Using the above equations, the thermoelectric figure of merit of a material can be expressed as
(9)
ZT =
S 2
G e
T
κ
p h
+
κ e
.
In our simulations, we treat both phonons and electrons as free particles, and thus the phonon contribution to thermoelectric power caused by phonon-drag is naturally ignored. Indeed, recent studies show that, at temperatures T > 10
K , the contribution to thermoelectric power in graphene mainly comes from diffusive processes and the phonon-drag effect can be ignored [32,33].

First, we consider the GNRs with zigzag edges in the presence of the narrowest constrictions. The structure is shown in Fig. 1(a). Fig. 2 (a) shows the phonon transmission of a ZGNR (
N z
= 8 ) with the narrowest constrictions (thick line). As a comparison, the result for the pristine nanoribbon is also shown (thin line). The transmission of the pristine ZGNR is staircase-like, with the value corresponding to the phonon channels at the given energy. In the presence of constrictions, the phonon transmission is suppressed largely at most phonon energies except very low ones since low energy phonons have long wavelength and thus can hardly be scattered by the constrictions. The suppressed phonon transmission can lead to low thermal conductance, as shown in Fig. 2(b). At temperature T = 300
K , the thermal conductance of the ZGNR with constrictions is about 5 ×
10
− 10
W / K , much lower than that of the pristine ZGNR, which is about 26 ×
10
− 10
W / K . The inset of Fig. 2(b) plots the thermal conductances of ZGNRs with the narrowest constrictions when the width (
N z
) of the nanoribbon is varied. Since the width
N c
of constrictions keeps unchanged (in fact, being the narrowest), we can see that the thermal conductance almost does not change regardless of the width of the nanoribbon
N z
. However, as the width of constrictions
N c
increases, the thermal conductance will increase accordingly as discussed below.

Fig. 3 shows the thermoelectric properties of a ZGNR (
N z
= 14 ) with the narrowest constrictions. Due to the particle–hole symmetry, ZT is symmetric about μ = 0 , and we only display the results for μ ⩾ 0 in Fig. 3(a). We can see that ZT exhibits a main peak value which corresponds a large variation of electron transmission with energy [see Fig. 3(c)]. The main peak ZT value, occurring at μ = 0.83
eV , exceeds one. Similarly, the thermopower S also shows a main peak value. The peak value is about 400 μV/K, which is similar to those obtained for other graphene systems [17,20]. In Fig. 3(d), the electron thermal conductance is shown. We see that, around μ = 0.83
eV , the electron thermal conductance increases abruptly from nearly zero to about 30 ×
10
− 10
W / K . Note that the phonon thermal conductance in this case is about 5 ×
10
− 10
W / K [see the inset of Fig. 2(b)]; thus the thermal conductance may become electron-dominated when the chemical potential is high.

The constrictions in the above discussions are kept to be the narrowest. In fact, the widths of the constrictions can affect both the electron transport and, in particular, the phonon transport largely. Fig. 4 (a) shows the maximal ZT value (
ZT max
) as a function of the constriction width (
N c
/
N z
) in a ZGNR with
N z
= 14 , while Figs. 4(b) and (c) show the corresponding
S 2
G e
and the thermal conductance, respectively. Roughly, the maximal ZT value reduces with increasing width of the constriction. At
N c
/
N z
= 0.2 , a sharp drop of
ZT max
appears, which can be ascribed to the large reduction of
S 2
G e
as shown in Fig. 4(b). Except at
N c
/
N z
= 0.2 ,
S 2
G e
does not change much as the constriction width
N c
increases. However, the phonon thermal conductance increases gradually with increasing
N c
[34]. At
N c
/
N z
= 1 (the pristine nanoribbon), the phonon thermal conductance is up to 60 ×
10
− 10
W / K , and the electron thermal conductance is also as high as 45 ×
10
− 10
W / K . The large thermal conductance leads to a low maximal ZT of the pristine ZGNR (
ZT max
= 0.1 ).

Since the ZGNR with the narrowest constrictions can have a higher maximal ZT value, we show the thermoelectric properties in Fig. 5 for a selection of ZGNRs with the narrowest constrictions. As a comparison, the results of the corresponding pristine ZGNRs are also shown. Roughly,
S 2
G e
of the ZGNR with constrictions is comparable to that of the corresponding pristine ZGNR [see Fig. 5(b)]. However, the thermal conductance of the ZGNR with constrictions is much lower than that of the pristine nanoribbon. This low thermal conductance thus leads to a higher ZT value for the ZGNR with constrictions. Indeed, the maximal value can exceed unity, depending on the specific structure. In addition, it is worth pointing out that in the presence of the narrowest constrictions the shape of the central dot is not much relevant. In fact, we have examined the rectangular dot and find that the maximal ZT can still be above unity.

Next, we study the thermoelectric properties of AGNRs with constrictions. In Fig. 6 , we show the phonon thermal conductance for an AGNR (
N a
= 11 ) with the narrowest constrictions (full line). In the presence of constrictions, the thermal conductance is much lower than that of the pristine nanoribbon (dashed line). At T = 300
K , the thermal conductance of the AGNR with the narrowest constrictions is about 5 ×
10
− 10
W / K (see the inset), which is similar to the result for the ZGNR with constrictions since the widths of constrictions are similar. The thermal conductance changes little with increasing width of the AGNR as the constrictions remain unchanged (the narrowest).

Fig. 7 shows the thermoelectric properties of the AGNR when the widths of the constrictions are changed. As the constriction widths increase, the maximal ZT value drops sharply as shown in Fig. 7(a). In fact, from Fig. 7(b) we observe that
S 2
G e
does not decrease too much, and it even increases at
N c
/
N a
= 1 (the pristine AGNR). The reduction of maximal ZT is mainly due to the rapid increase of thermal conductance with increasing widths of the constrictions as shown in Fig. 7(c). This behavior is similar to the case of ZGNRs with constrictions.

In Fig. 8 (a), we show the maximal ZT values for several AGNRs with the narrowest constrictions. The results for the corresponding pristine AGNRs are also shown. For the AGNRs with constrictions, the maximal ZT value changes a lot as
N a
is varied, which is due to the big variation of
S 2
G e
with
N a
since the thermal conductance remains almost constant [see Figs. 8(b) and (c)]. In the case of pristine AGNRs,
S 2
G e
and the thermal conductance almost linearly increase with
N a
, while the maximal ZT value slightly increases with
N a
. In addition, due to the higher thermal conductances, the maximal ZT values of the pristine AGNRs are much lower than those of the AGNRs with constrictions.

In summary, we have investigated the thermoelectric properties of a range of graphene nanoribbons in the presence of two constrictions. Thus, the structure can be also viewed as a hexagonal graphene quantum dot connected to two leads. We have shown that, with decreasing widths of the constrictions, the thermal conductance of a nanoribbon can be reduced largely while
S 2
G e
(power factor) remains still high as compared with the results for the pristine nanoribbon. As a result, the thermoelectric figure of merit of the nanoribbon can be enhanced greatly. In the presence of narrowest constrictions, the ZT values of the zigzag quantum dots can exceed one (or even be up to two) while the ZT values of the armchair quantum dots can be close to one, depending on the size of the dot. These results indicate that quantum confined structure of graphene may be promising for thermoelectric applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q.L. was supported by NSFC (Grant No. 10904092). H.Z. was partially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 10904112).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum Kinetics in Transport and Optics of Semiconductors
